segment-text: INT. EMILY'S APARTMENT - A SERIES OF STILLS

ANGLE ON BOTTLE OF PRESCRIPTION PILLS Next to an unmade bed. ANGLE ON A TOPPLED REMOTE CONTROL SAILBOAT Broken mast on the floor. sail. A bloody handprint on the ANGLE ON A TABLE SET FOR THREE The stick of butter is rounded from melting. A water glass lies on it's side. A fly on the butter. ANGLE ON THE FRONT DOOR Desperate scratches on the lower third with blood stain filigree-- leading up to the door knob. The room is upside down in the reflection. Such is the trick of surfaces.
INT. TRIBECA LOFT - KITCHEN - MORNING

ANGLE ON A LITTLE BOY WITH HIS HAND OUT Like he's waiting for the Eucharist. He is EZRA BANKS (6). A GUMMIE VITE AND MULTI-VITAMIN are dropped in his hand. It is offered up by JONATHAN BANKS (30's). He and his wife DIERDRE (30's) are making breakfast.
OK, here we go. 100% of everything we need to make it until tomorrow.
His phone comes to life.
(to the phone) Dr. Banks.
Banks answers.
OK, E, try and get more in your mouth and less on the floor.
Whatever he is told shifts the morning and all the mornings to come. He moves into the next room.
Yeah...She's my patient...
He hangs up. Staggered by the news.
What? What's wrong?
He shrugs and leaves. Confidentiality.
INT. NYC TAXI - MOMENTS LATER
Anxious and on the phone.
Sorry to wake you. It's Jon Banks... It's Emily... ANGLE ON ERICA SIEBERT In her house-- getting dressed.
God, I'm so sorry, John. Yes, yes... I'll come into the city.
EXT. NYC - CONTINUOUS
Banks gets out of the cab and runs up the steps of a
Manhattan Police Station. INT. POLICE STATION - CONTINUOUS
A busy morning. COPS AND CIVILIANS milling about. approaches the DESK SERGEANT.
I'm here regarding Emily Hawkins. I was called.
(checking the log) You a lawyer? Psychiatrist.
EXT. OTISVILLE PRISON - DAY
Emily Hawkins (late 20's and very pretty) assesses her face in the rear view of a 10 year old Mercedes.
INT. OTISVILLE PRISON - WAITING ROOM
Emily Hawkins approaches the guard at the desk.
My husband... Martin Hawkins.

Emily sits and waits. She clocks another woman about the same age. The woman is AGITATED-- pacing. Emily looks at her empathetically. Mirror neurons firing. ANGLE ON EMILY She goes through a metal detector. her under wire bra has tripped it. confronts her.
That got metal in it? metal.
BEEP. A scan reveal A FEMALE GUARD Can't have ANGLE ON EMILY She removes her bra and leaves it in a locker in a dressing room.
INT. OTISVILLE VISITING ROOM - EMILY AND MARTIN
Emily sits with Martin. Not much privacy.
I bet you're not gonna miss the drive, or the ambience.
I'd have gone a lot further.
They gave me a brochure on reintegrating. Social worker came by.
Yeah, what did he say? Go slow.
We can go slow.
EXT. OTISVILLE PRISON - A LITTLE LATER
Emily sees the woman from the waiting room sobbing on the bench.
(soothing) Hey, you OK?
SOBBING looks up at her.
Not getting parole. That's what the lawyer said... Another year. Wasn't ready for that.
Emily sits down and hugs the woman.
How long has it been?
Two years. I just can't... I can't keep coming here.
I know how that feels.
How long for you?
I shouldn't be complaining.
She gets rummages in her purse. Gets out a bottle of pills. Shakes out a pill and swallows it dry. Offers one to Emily.
EXT. NYC - DAY
The Manhattan skyline patrolled by heavy gray clouds. Dead leaves float in puddles. Bare branches scratch at the sky.
MONTAGE - CREDIT SEQUENCE - DAY

NEW YORKERS relying on what lies beyond a childproof cap to get through the day. Cuts of the following... -A KID TAKES RITALIN IN THE MORNING WITH HIS BREAKFAST. -A MIDDLE AGED MAN PUTS CIALIS IN HIS GYM BAG AND KISSES HIS WIFE GOODBYE. -A WOMAN WAITS IN A LONG LINE AT A PHARMACY. -A MAN ON AN AIRPLANE TAKES A VALIUM AND BREATHES DEEPLY. -A BUS SIDE OFFERS A PILL FOR HAIR LOSS. -A PEN ON A PRESCRIPTION PAD.
INT. BANKS OFFICE - EVENING

Danish modern furniture, a little mismatched. A couple of bookshelves loaded down with periodicals and textbooks. Some abstract art on the wall. The pen is held by Dr. Banks-- it is a promo from a drug company. He gives the prescription to a NERVOUS PATIENT (male, 20's) in his office.
Nervous. Takes the prescription and sighs.
(smiles) Ready to go meet some people?
Nervous nods. Banks smiles.
INT. BANKS OFFICE - LATER
Banks leaves-- through a cracked door he sees one of his partners is reading. JEAN CHARNLEY (60's and wise). She listens to classical music.
You're still here.
I have a 7 PM. You headed home? BANKS Hospital. Oh, the rep from Pfizer... Tracey called. She wants to know if we're free for a meal this week.
Who am I to say "no" to free food. BANKS Will you let Ellis know?
Free food with Cheryl Lynn from Pfizer! Writing it down! Banks leaves.
The Bitter Pill 11-22-11 EXT. OTISVILLE CORRECTIONAL FACILITY - MORNING
Emily and an older woman, MARTHA (60), wait by the car. Martin emerges from the front doors of the facility. Martin reaches the two women.
You look thin... Em... Mom...
The three embrace in a big group hug.
Let's get out of here.
INT. BEDROOM - NIGHT

Emily on her back in bed. Martin on top of her. A lot of time and regret being sorted out in long sexual strokes. Emily wraps her legs around him, pulls him in deeper. Martin slows to a stop and collapses next to her. Her face turns toward the window -- her eyes focus on something far away.
I guess maybe that wasn't slow.
She puts her head on his chest-- smiles.
I can't believe you're back.
She goes to the bathroom. Closes the door.

(through the door) There was a guy I met in...uh...a guy who was in for some tax stuff. Went to Stanford-- brilliant guy. Mark Hellman-- he's gonna be out soon. He's got money in Dubai and a lot of connections. We're gonna get back to where we were, Em. I can make that happen for you.
The Bitter Pill 11-22-11 INT. NYC PARKING GARAGE - A LITTLE LATER

Emily, dressed for work, walks toward her car. She pauses for a moment and checks her purse for the keys. In the process her bag slips from her shoulder. The contents dump out on the cement floor of the garage. Cars move around her -- one honks. RAMON, the attendant, rushes over to help her. the brink of tears. A little terrified. She's on
Thank you, Ramon.
INT. EMILY'S CAR - CONTINUOUS
She puts her seatbelt on. Starts the car.
EMILY'S POV

She glides from her parking space. Tilting down to the speedometer -- she is going ten miles an hour. She accelerates. The exit ramp bends to the left but Emily goes straight. In front of her is a brick wall. The car holds its speed. Emily drives directly into the wall. From inside the car we see THE AIR BAG EXPLODE violently.
EXT. NYC PARKING GARAGE - CONTINUOUS

The front of the car accordions back toward the passenger compartment, collapsing the crumple zones. A hubcap rolls away from the wreck. A streak of blood across the marshmallow white of the air bags. Ramon and a couple of other BYSTANDERS come running over, already dialing their cell phones. The Bitter Pill
11-22-11 INT. BELLEVUE HOSPITAL EMERGENCY ROOM - DAY
In a glassed-in room a MANACLED GANGBANGER sits in prison fatigues. DOCTOR JONATHAN BANKS (30s) looks at an X-RAY on a light box.
How'd it get in there?
Swallowed it. The gangbanger is confused by Banks' bedside manner.

When I was in medical school there was a guy who came in -- swallowed a size C battery. He said he felt he was running out of energy. Somehow he choked down one of those Duracells. Crazy, right? By the time he got to the ER it was leaking in his small intestine. There was actual electrical current going from anode to soft tissue to cathode. He died. The gangbanger nods.
Why'd you eat the battery?
Voice told me I could be supersonic.
Banks' pager goes off. He looks at the number -- smiles.
A voice? Interesting. Like me now? Did you know the voice? I don't know. voice.
It was just a And the voice said you should snack on a Triple A? It didn't say "snack."
Banks goes over to an EMERGENCY KIT attached to the wall. He opens the kit and sorts through it.

Well, the voice gave you bad information. See, if you want me to tell the District Attorney that you're too crazy to stand trial for whatever it is you did, then that voice should have told you to save the Triple A for your radio and go further into the alphabet; all the way to D.
He opens up a flashlight from the emergency kit. He takes out a hefty D battery and slams it on the table like a shot of tequila.
Why don't you and your voice talk it over. I'll be outside.
INT. HOSPITAL CORRIDOR - MOMENTS LATER
Banks walks into the hall. Goes to a fridge and gets a RED BULL. Opens it. A NURSE approaches.
The Ever-Ready Bunny in there is malingering. He gets a bed for
the night and then the bus back.
A NURSE hands him a file.
MVA with minor head trauma in need of a consult. And then off the Red Bull.
That stuff'll kill you.
Yeah, but at least I'll be awake when it happens.
The Bitter Pill INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - MOMENTS LATER

Emily sits on the edge of her bed, bruised but intact. One of her legs sticking out from under her gown. She stares out the window-- muttering to herself. Banks enters and looks at her chart. Mrs. Hawkins?
I'm Dr. Banks.
They said I may have a concussion. I don't need to stay here for that do I?
I don't think so.
She gets up and starts to organize her clothes.
Before you go, I do have a couple
of questions.
How is your head? Hurts.
How was it before the wall?
I don't understand.
How have things been going with you, generally speaking?
Why are you here? Banks reads the file.

I'm a psychiatrist, Mrs. Hawkins, and normally when people hit things with their car, there are skid marks on the pavement. (MORE) A brick wall is a good reason to use the brakes, turn the wheel. But you didn't do that did you? You went straight in. It says here that the attendant told the police you were upset before you got in the car. The insurance company is going to check the brakes, but I'd prefer to just ask you.
Emily looks away. She recedes into herself.
(sotto) I can't do this. BANKS What can't you do?
Emily gives him a long look -- a balloon about to burst.
I had-- I was treated for depression a few years ago. I thought with Martin coming back, it would just go away. Just somehow get over it like a normal person.
Her voice starts to crack.
It's all a brick wall.
Banks nods empathetically. Who's Martin?
My husband. He's been away. He's been... in prison. And I... I don't want to disappoint him and I did. Why am I like this? BANKS Emily, how do you feel about staying here for a few days?
Emily starts to panic.
Here? I can't stay here.
I think you tried to hurt yourself this morning and we need toEMILY No. I need to be there for him. We're starting over. We're putting this behind us-- it'll go away right. It's a mood swing. But-

And I have a job. I work in advertising and I need to go in. Marty doesn't make any money yet. What if I come see you? To your office. Do you have an office? Just don't make me stay here. Emily, what you did today isEMILY (off his wedding band) It was a mistake. I lost it for a minute. You're married, right? Imagine you come home from jail-or from being away for a long time and you find your wife in a mental hospital. I promise I won't do anything like that again. We waited for three years, Dr. Banks. I'll come as often as you want-two or three times a week. I remember that-- structure helps with hopelessness. I was in therapy before-- this morning was a mistake.
Banks eyes her. She seems scared as a child.
INT. HOSPITAL WAITING ROOM - LATER
Banks speaks to Martin and Martha.
Call me if she talks about hurting herself. I want to see her at my office tomorrow. That was the deal we made.
I had no idea this was still going on with her.
I think she was putting on a brave face, Marty. Afraid to tell you. Afraid to tell me.
I want her to start on this-- it's called an SSRI. It's a medicine that makes it harder for the brain to tell you that you're sad.
Banks hands them a card and a PRESCRIPTION.
INT. CVS PHARMACY - LATER
Emily and Martin. She picks up her meds.

You want to try and take this with food and at the same time every day. Side Effects may include agitation, weight loss, nausea, headaches, insomnia-- you'll want to call your Doctor if those become a problem.
INT. LE CIRQUE RESTAURANT - DAY
Banks is at lunch with Charnley and AARON ELLIS (40s)-and a pretty young pharmaceutical rep, TRACEY SUTTON
I remember one year the rep from Warner Lambert took 25 of us to Bermuda to play golf for a weekend. Spouses included.
Glaxo gave me tickets to the Lion King, the Ballet-- took my nephew to the world series. Where was I for all of that?
In high school, probably.
That was before my time, too. But, I'm still allowed to buy you doctors lunch. As long as we talk about business for at least 5 minutes.
(off his watch) Ready? Go.
We're starting a trial for our new anti-anxiety drug: Serenex. We're looking for a couple of Docs to consult. It's probably gonna run for about a year. Any interest?
I'm out. I'm doing a thing for AstraZeneca. Signed a noncompete.
She should take away your filet for that. I'm teaching next semester-- feeling a little overextended.
What about you, Dr. Banks? Too busy?
Not necessarily too busy. you need?

Well, I can have the head of the study get in touch with you about protocols and all that. That would be awesome if you could participate. I can't take you to Bermuda for golf, but we do pay consulting fees.
INT. TRIBECA LOFT - NIGHT
Littered with moving boxes. ANGLE ON THE DINING ROOM TABLE Banks and Dierdre have a glass of wine. BANKS Getting warmer.
Thirty thousand? BANKS
And what do you have to do? BANKS Go to a few meetings, recruit some patients, keep track of some dataDIERDRE So, you'll be even busier?
He pulls her on top of the table. Takes off her shirt.
Yeah, but we'll be able to afford a bed.
The bed comes on Friday.
She starts to laugh. He shifts to gain a better angle and knocks a wine glass on the floor.
Oh, shit. Shhh...
A light goes on in the hallway.
Dad???
They scurry to get decent. It's OK, E.
Just unpacking.
Ezra walks into the hallway.
I was having a bad dream.
Well, you're in luck. Your Dad is really good with dreams.
Banks leads the boy back toward his room.
Do I have to dream when I sleep?

Yeah, I'm pretty sure we do, E. But, they're not always bad. Albert Einstein figured out relativity in a dream. And Paul McCartney dreamed up entire songs. I dreamt about you before you even got here.
INT. YOUNG AND RUBICAM ADVERTISING - DAY

Emily sits at her desk outside the office of her boss-ILENE SPIVAK (40s). EMILY GETS UP FROM HER DESK AND KNOCKS OVER HER COFFEE She stumbles past the work stations. Her hand goes up to her mouth as though she is going to be sick. A CO-WORKER tries to help her. Emily!
Are you OK?
She runs toward the bathroom.
INT. ILENE'S OFFICE - SIMULTANEOUSLY
Ilene is on the phone. The co-worker pokes her head in. CO-WORKER Something's wrong with Emily. The Bitter Pill
11-22-11 INT. BATHROOM - CONTINUOUS
Ilene enters to find the water running at one sink and the sound of Emily sobbing quietly in a stall. On the edge of the sink is a PRESCRIPTION BOTTLE. Ilene inspects the bottle: PAXIL. Emily?
You OK?
Emily collects herself and emerges.
I'm so sorry. I just felt sick all of a sudden.
Ilene hands her the pill bottle.
(off the pills) They made me sick, too. I had better luck with Celexa. BANKS (O.S.) (prelap) How did you meet?
INT. BANKS' OFFICE - DAY
Emily sits on the sofa. Banks sits on a high-backed chair across from her. Awkward silence. She stares at MONET POSTER on the wall-- WATER LILIES.
I like your poster. paint like that... Do you paint? I wanted to

I used to draw when I was a kid. Mainly people. Teachers and kids from my class. Boys I liked and sometimes cats. I tried to get their faces perfect. That's why I came here. I wanted to get into graphic design. I was taking classes when I met Martin. BANKS How did that happen?

I worked at a bar downtown to pay for school. He kept coming in after work and ordering the most complicated drinks-- things I had no idea how to make. Like a Dark and Stormy, was the name of one. Anyway, one night he climbed over the bar and offered to teach me and ended up staying until we closed. Then he asked me out.
Banks smiles at the story.

I didn't know who he was, or what he did. He was just this guy in a suit who watched me while I made drinks and it made me nervous. And he stared at me like I was a painting. He had a beautiful sailboat and he took me out on it-we went down to the Bahamas for a week that fall. Then we went to Aspen and he taught me how to ski. He swept me off my feet. Proposed to me in Maui that winter and we were married by spring. BANKS How old were you?
That was like 5 years ago-- so I was 23. Time goes so fast-- and then it just stops and doesn't
move. How does that happen? She starts to get dark.
You said you were depressed before.
She nods.
It's worse this time. I don't know how to get it off me. It's like a giant thumb pushing me down.

We've done studies on depression. And here's what we've learned. Some people benefit from talking about their thoughts and feelings; trying to heal wounds in their psyche. Maybe by understanding some pain from the past we can resolve it-- let it go. How we think can actually change our brain chemistry.
I told all the stories about my past the last time and I'm still sad. Maybe it doesn't matter what I think. Maybe my brain is telling me to be sad.
Other people benefit from taking medications that act directly on the brain. But, the people who do the best are the ones who do both-talking and medication. That's what I do here.
So, you want me to take drugs?
We call it medication. But, yes, I think that would help you. Have you thought about hurting yourself again?
I wouldn't do that to Martin.
You said you felt this way before. Did you see someone about it the last time?
Emily nods.
In Connecticut. Greenwich. Erica Siebert. Then I moved back to the city when they took away our house.
Do you mind if I call Dr. Siebert about your history?
If you think that'll help. I just want to get better.
She hands him back her bottle of pills.
These aren't working. They made me sick to my stomach.
Sometimes that happens. I'm gonna try you on something else instead...
INT. PHARMACY - LATER
Emily stands in line with a NUMBER OF OTHER PEOPLE. faces in food line.
We'll find something that takes the edge off the sadness. We'll get you through this.
Emily's name is called. The PHARMACIST hands her a bottle.
OK, I just want to make you aware of the side effects...
Emily nods.
INT. NYC TAXI - DUSK
BANKS' POV A CRAZY MAN in the street is having an animated conversation with a FIRE HYDRANT. Banks puts up the window to silence his rant.
(pre-lap) I saw her four years ago. For a little over 9 months...
INT. MIDTOWN RESTAURANT - EVENING
Banks sits with Siebert. She wears a NAME TAG for a professional conference. Nearby is a kiosk announcing another NEW DRUG: PARADOL. It's a MOOD STABILIZER. The commercial for it plays in a loop.

She didn't just have the rug pulled out from under her-- the rug, the house, the husband-- her entire life. All gone-- including her health insurance. She moved into the city to find work. Never heard from her again. I'm glad she's seeing a man this time, I think that'll help. Why is that?
Never felt seen by her father, then her husband ends up in jail and she's abandoned again. It'd be good for her to have a different kind of experience. Banks takes this in.
She said you tried her on antidepressants, but she had a hard
time tolerating the meds. Siebert consults her notes.
Tried her on Prozac, Wellbutrin and Effexor. Really struggled. Problems with nausea, sleep-headaches. BANKS I'm putting her on Zoloft-- see if she can tolerate that.
Maybe she's a candidate for one of these newer meds. Something glutamate based. I've had some success with those. Yeah, maybe. We'll see how the Zoloft goes.
A PRETTY DRUG REP chimes the side of a wine glass.
We'll be starting the presentation soon-- so if I can get everyone to
come in and take a seat. OTHER DOCS begin to file into a private dining room.
You sure you don't want to stay? BANKS I do a few nights a week at Bellevue. I should get going. Thanks so much for taking the time.
Not at all. But, I am wondering if you could give me a little advice.

I'm looking for space here in the city. I don't know what your work situation is, but if you hear of anything could you let me know. Just a couple afternoons a week. BANKS Sure. I can ask my partners. There's other practices in our building.
He gives her a card.
That's great. Thanks so much. I, uh, I'm recently single and need to get back out into the world. Greenwich is a little small.
She looks at all the Doctors filing into the room.
Did Emily's husband ever get out of prison? A week ago.
Maybe that'll help turn things around.
EXT. CENTRAL PARK CONSERVATORY POND - MORNING
Emily sits on a bench and watches a LITTLE BOY deep in concentration with his RC sailboat. She seems lighter.
That's called a run-- when the wind is behind you. You want to let the sail out.
Do you have a boat?
No...not anymore. But, I will again someday. Did it sink?
LITTLE BOY
Yeah. Something like that. It can happen if you're not careful.
Martha walks up. Notices Emily with the little girl.
Making friends? And just as quickly it's gone -- a wave of sadness passes back over Emily.
(to Little Boy) I have to go.
She and Martha walk away.
Martin told me what happened with you. The car.
Emily nods. Martha hugs her.
Everyone has a dark place. I barely know a soul who hasn't been in therapy or at least should have been. Did anything like this run in your family?
I don't remember my Mom getting depressed-- but she left when I was pretty young. I don't remember my Dad ever being anything but angry.
I went through a really bad case of the blues after Martin was born. Post partum. Took me a while to get over it. But, I did. It just went away over time.
I keep thinking that if I just smile and pretend it'll all change. You know, be positive. Try and change my chemistry with my thoughts.
Martha touches her cheek.
This is the last thing he needs.
What do you mean-- "This?" Me.
Depressed.
I read an article about herbs that are supposed to help. St. John's Wort. I brought you some. In Germany they use herbs as much as drugs.
Martha gives Emily a small apothecary bottle.
Has Martin mentioned anything to you about his new plan?
The only plan I know of is to show you and everyone else that he's changed.
INT. BANKS OFFICE - EVENING
Banks and Emily.

Angry? No. Never. About what? It's such a slippery slope once you get started. You know? How much is enough? We were on a path-big house, boat, talking about children. A second house, a third car. I didn't come from money-Milwaukee isn't Greenwich. There are people out there who do things much worse than what Marty did-they cut down rainforests or run sweatshops somewhere and they're getting rich. They live next out there, too. You see them on the weekends on the tennis courts or in the restaurants. With their kids at the yacht club. He was trying to keep up. He was trying to please me.
Martin broke the law, though. He risked your safety. How did you feel about that?
Beat. Emily squeezes a Kleenex in her fist and pounds her leg gently. Banks clocks this.

Everyone was cheating-- I mean look at what they did to the country. Martin just did what everyone else was doing. Only he got caught. It doesn't make it right, but he did it for me. He was trying to take care of me-he's the only one who ever did that. What about your father?

He did what he could-- he worked at a bottling plant. He didn't know how stocks worked or how money gets made. He took me to Chicago on vacation and I wanted to go to the Art Institute and see the paintings. The ones they put on postcards... but he just wanted to go to the baseball game and it was so hot out... I wanted to go to the beach. To go swimming...
She starts to cry.
Banks makes a note. She tries to gather herself.
He didn't like my swimming suit. He thought it was too sexy. So, I had to stay inside. I had all sorts of issues about my body. Martin was the first person I felt pretty around...God, my head hurts.
Do you think it's from the medication?
She nods.
I don't think I can keep taking these.
INT. SWIMMIMG POOL - NIGHT
Banks and Dierdre watch Ezra at a swim meet. pool is crowded with kids and parents.
The indoor
OK, E, I think the relay is after this one.
A YOUNG GIRL nearby drops her towel and gets ready to swim-- arching her back and stretching. Her swimsuit isn't at all revealing-- but still... Banks clocks her.
swimsuit, D?
Me, God, I don't know. I'm sure it had polka dots on it or something. BANKS How many pieces?
(flirty) One. But, I probably wanted it to be two.
I think it's my turn.
OK, Michael Phelps-- be like a shark out there.
I don't want be a shark, I want to be tuna.
Dierdre walks Ezra over to the starting blocks. DAD in a suit approaches Banks. Hey, John. A SINGLE SINGLE DAD
Miles, how are you?
Not bad, busy with work. Hey, I'm wondering if you can help me out. We have Alex on Ritalin and we left the bottle at his Mom's. I really don't want to go back there tonight-- just too much drama.
Yeah, I understand.
I have him for the rest of the week, so just enough for a few days. 20 Milligrams. You want me to phone it in?
He gets out his phone.
INT. EMILY'S APARTMENT - LATER
Emily stands in the bathroom applying eye make-up. is very dressed up. She
You remember Carl Millbank? His wife is on the board at the Guggenheim. She had extra tickets. Carl still feels shitty about everything-- wants to try and make up.
I haven't seen any of those people in so long.
Look, don't mention anything to Carl about that guy Hellman. It'll just make things easier.
The door opens and Emily appears holding the pill bottle. She drops her dose on the floor.
God, these things make me dizzy.
If you don't feel up to it I can go by myself. I understand if you don't want to see those people. But, I gotta get back out there at some point.
No, if I just sit here that'll make me feel even worse. I need to try. Dr. Banks would want me to try.
EXT. CIRCLE LINE CRUISE - GUGGENHEIM BENEFIT - DUSK
The FINANCE AND INVESTMENT CROWD eat tray pass as a jazz trio plays on GIANT YACHT. Emily and Martin speak to CARL MILLBANK (50s) and his wife KAYLA (30s). Emily shifts uncomfortably. Anxious.
Hey, Martin. Emily. you. Welcome back. Carl. Kayla. wife Emily.
Good to see
You remember my
Thank you for inviting us. I think I saw your dress in a magazine. It's better on you.
So sweet. I'm glad you could come.
It's been so long since I had to get dressed up.
Emily is trying to keep a brave face-- but she is brittle. She notices Kayla's drink.
I'm going to the bar -- can I get something for someone?
Is that a good idea?
It's the only idea I have right now.
She makes her way through the crowd toward the rail of the boat. Martin clocks her, concerned.
Emily looks like she lost weight.
Yeah, she's struggling a bit. Going away is hard-- and so is coming back. For everyone. (MORE)
It means a lot to both of us, this invitation.
I wish I had done a better job of reaching out. I really do.
Hey, it's the culture. It's over now.
I called Em a few times, but she sort of drifted away. I thought she might have moved back to the Midwest. Milwaukee? Minneapolis?
She's been in the city working-advertising. I'm really proud of her.
EMILY AT THE RAIL
A big tear comes down her cheek. heeled. Looks out at the water. She surveys the well- Kayla walks up.
(off the tear) Em? Oh, sweetheart. What's wrong?
Emily just stares. You OK? Hopeless.
I can't do this.
You know, I went through a real bad time last year. I found a lump last year. I just fell apart. So depressed. My doctor put me on something called Effexor. It really helped.
Emily holds onto her arm for a moment -- searching for a connection.
